claims.text: In late 1992 a Chinese underground nuclear detonation sent shock waves to Alaska that revealed a 550-foot pyramid buried 100 feet beneath central Alaska, bigger than the Great Pyramid of Giza.

evidence.quote:
shock waves in the Earth's crust all the
way to
Alaska

a huge pyramid
bigger than the Great Pyramid of Giza
550 ft tall

claims.rationale: The Dark Pyramid story originates with Doug Muchler's 2012 interview with Linda Moulton Howe; no seismic, geophysical, or imaging data supports a 550-foot buried pyramid in central Alaska. Host later casts doubt on the story in his verdict section (c_0013). Low directTruth but the claim is faithfully reported.

claims.text: The local Alaska news report about the underground pyramid was pulled from the 11 p.m. broadcast after men in suits grilled the station manager, and all tapes were confiscated.

evidence.quote:
the late night news came and went
without a single mention of the pyramid

the story was
true

claims.rationale: Story rests on Muchler's account of an anonymous station employee's admission. No independent corroboration from the named station, no FOIA record, no former newsroom staff going on record. Single-source whistleblower testimony 20 years after the fact; plausible only if the cover-up was wildly successful.

claims.text: The US military knows exactly where the dark pyramid is and is the one operating it.

evidence.quote:
the military knows exactly
where it is they also know what the
pyramid is

claims.rationale: The claim chains entirely on Muchler's men-in-black warehouse encounter anecdote. No documentary evidence, no leaked program name, no budget trail. The 'they don't want anyone messing with them up there' formulation is the sort of post-hoc dramatic construction that whistleblower accounts acquire over retellings. Low directTruth.

dependencies.rationale: There must be a real pyramid for the military to be operating it.

claims.text: According to a helicopter pilot, the Dark Pyramid is made of black stone, is thousands of years old, and generates enough power to light the entire country.

evidence.quote: thousands of years old

it's a power generator of some kind
and this thing puts out a lot of
juice

claims.rationale: Second-hand story (pilot → Lee Pearson → son Bruce → Howe). No power-grid records show an anomalous baseload source in Alaska. National-grid scale generation requires visible infrastructure (cooling, transmission) absent from any aerial or satellite imagery of interior Alaska. Near-zero directTruth.

claims.text: Marty Johnson's son says his father built an aluminum replica of the Alaska pyramid that produced enough electricity to power their entire house and farm from a small input voltage.

evidence.quote:
Marty Johnson's
son said his father built a replica of
the pyramid

this mini pyramid produced
more than enough electricity to power
their entire house and
farm

claims.rationale: This would violate conservation of energy: an aluminum pyramid energized with 'a small amount of voltage' cannot output net power to a house and farm (thermodynamics second law). Either a free-energy machine, which does not exist, or the son misremembers / the story is embellished. Very low directTruth.

dependencies.rationale: A working miniature would strengthen the full-scale pyramid-as-power-plant hypothesis.

claims.text: Bruce Pearson flew over Denali and spotted remains of an old road and airstrip that don't appear on any maps, roughly 100 miles from the nearest marked road.

evidence.quote:
they found the remains of
an Old Road a road that doesn't appear
on any maps

they found an old Air
strip this also does not appear on any
Maps

claims.rationale: Interior Alaska has genuine legacy airstrips from WWII/Cold War-era bush and military operations that postdate common mapping — 'not on any map' is a loose standard since many are absent from Google Maps but known to locals. Without coordinates and imagery, claim is plausible but unverifiable.

dependencies.rationale: Physical signs of human activity in remote Denali are consistent with a military-operated facility.

claims.text: In May 2020 Nathan Campbell hired a plane to fly him to Kenai Lake in Denali National Park to search for the dark pyramid; his campsite was later found abandoned, with the last diary entry reading 'went to get water,' and he was never found.

evidence.quote:
in May 2020 Nathan Campbell hired a
plane

claims.rationale: Alaska does see hundreds of missing persons cases annually (the 'Alaska Triangle' trope is built on real disappearance statistics). I cannot independently verify the specific Nathan Campbell case — it may be real or a narrative the host absorbed uncritically. Half credit pending verification.

dependencies.rationale: A disappearance near the alleged pyramid site is consistent with the cover-up narrative — though Alaska disappearances are common for mundane reasons.

claims.text: Project Stargate remote viewer Pat Price was able to see an underground base hidden inside a mountain in the middle of the Alaska triangle — Mount Hayes.

evidence.quote:
Pat was able to to
see an underground base hidden inside a
mountain

claims.rationale: Pat Price was a real CIA-contracted remote viewer in Project SCANATE/Stargate (1970s-80s) and is well-documented. However, remote viewing as a protocol failed to produce replicated empirical results under the AIR 1995 review, so the 'he saw it' framing treats remote-viewing hits as evidence of physical facts. Most Pat Price hits are retroactively matched to already-known sites.

claims.text: A remote viewer scanned the Dark Pyramid for Linda Moulton Howe and saw it as an energy device functioning as a charging station for UFOs, operated by a non-human tour guide.

evidence.quote:
she saw a huge black
sloped wall she pulled away a
bit it was the dark pyramid

it is an energy device it's
a charging station for
UFOs

claims.rationale: Claim combines remote-viewing methodology (not empirically validated) with a single anonymous witness and an extraordinary non-human tour-guide claim. Unverifiable by design — the claimed protocol produces only mental imagery, with no external referent. Minimum directTruth.

dependencies.rationale: If there is no Dark Pyramid, there is nothing to remote-view.

claims.text: Over 6,500 UFO sightings have been reported in the Alaska triangle, making it one of the most active UFO hotspots in the world.

evidence.quote:
there have been over 6,500 UFO
sightings in the Alaska triangle

claims.rationale: Alaska does have notable UAP reporting to NUFORC and MUFON, but 6,500 in one region is high and likely counts broad Alaska rather than the 'triangle' specifically. The 'Alaska Triangle' is itself largely a cryptid-media construct rather than an officially recognized region. Figure cites no source.

dependencies.rationale: High UFO-sighting volume in the Alaska triangle is the supposed observational basis for the 'pyramid as charging station' hypothesis.

claims.text: Doug Muchler produced his DD Form 214 to Linda Moulton Howe, demonstrating he is genuinely a former Military Intelligence officer.

evidence.quote:
Doug
emailed her she got copies of his ID
addresses and phone numbers

claims.rationale: Howe does report having seen Muchler's DD-214 on earthfiles.com. Credentials-genuine is the most substantiated piece of the whole story; what he did in that role and whether he witnessed what he claims is separate. Credentials verified ≠ narrative verified.

dependencies.rationale: Verified service credentials strengthen (but do not prove) Muchler's cornerstone testimony about the suppressed news story.

claims.text: China did detonate an underground nuclear test on May 22nd 1992, five to six months earlier than Doug Muchler's claimed late-1992 news broadcast.

evidence.quote:
Chinese did detonate an underground nuke
on May 22nd 1992

that's 5 or 6
months earlier than Doug claims

claims.rationale: China's Lop Nor test of May 21, 1992 (660kt yield) is well-documented. The 5-6 month gap is straightforward subtraction from Muchler's claimed 'late November / early December 1992' timeline. Host uses this to undermine the dark-pyramid story.

dependencies.rationale: [logical] If the only known Chinese underground nuke in 1992 was in May, not November, Muchler's late-1992 news story cannot be about that event.

claims.text: There was a major 5.3 earthquake in Egypt in October 1992 that killed at least 500 people and drew UN attention and sustained news coverage, which Doug Muchler may be conflating with his pyramid memory.

evidence.quote:
there was a major earthquake in Egypt in
October 1992
the earthquake was a 5.3

claims.rationale: The October 12, 1992 Cairo earthquake (Mw 5.8, source-reported 5.3-5.9 range) killed 545+ and generated sustained coverage about threats to pyramids and the Sphinx. Host offers this as a memory-conflation hypothesis for why Muchler recalls 'news about pyramids' from late 1992.

dependencies.rationale: [alternative] A plausible mundane source for a late-1992 pyramid news memory removes the need for a suppressed Alaska-pyramid story.

claims.text: Military-intelligence whistleblowers including Richard Doty, Christopher Mellon, Luis Elizondo, David Grusch, and now Doug Muchler form a pattern where Military Intelligence sources repeatedly seed UFO-adjacent narratives.

evidence.quote:
Doug muchler is lying but I do view his
story skeptically

claims.rationale: Richard Doty is a documented former AFOSI disinformation officer (admitted on-record). The other named whistleblowers (Mellon, Elizondo, Grusch) did work in DoD/CIA-adjacent intelligence roles. Pattern recognition is fair; attribution of deliberate disinfo across all five is stronger than the evidence supports. Host takes care to say 'not saying any one of them is lying'.

dependencies.rationale: [undercuts] If Military Intelligence whistleblowers are an unreliable source class, Muchler's cornerstone testimony is weakened.

claims.text: Linda Moulton Howe is a True Believer who enters a story believing it and investigates details to support it, rather than approaching stories skeptically.

evidence.quote:
she is a True Believer yes she does
her homework but she goes into a story
believing it

claims.rationale: Fair characterization grounded in Howe's own public-facing methodology across decades of UFO reporting (Strange Harvest, Coast to Coast, earthfiles.com). She leans toward confirmation of extraordinary claims over parsimony. Classifying her epistemic stance is a matter of reasoned judgment, not pure fact — hence opinion kind with moderately high directTruth.
